explanations.1: The text states that most heat transfer produced from burning fuel is released into the environment, implying high inefficiency.
explanations.2: The first law states the change in internal energy equals net heat transfer into the system minus the net work done by the system.
explanations.3: The text explains that ΔU is independent of the path or method used to reach a state, unlike Q and W.
explanations.4: Net heat transfer is the sum of heat in minus heat out, calculated as 40.00 J - 25.00 J = 15.00 J.
explanations.5: If intake matches losses to heat and work, the internal energy remains constant, meaning ΔU = 0.
explanations.6: Using ΔU = Q - W, -150 = Q - 30, which results in Q = -120 J, meaning 120 J left the system.
explanations.7: For an isobaric process, work is calculated as W = PΔV, which is (2.00 x 10^5)(0.003) = 600 J.
explanations.8: An isochoric process occurs at constant volume, meaning ΔV = 0 and therefore W = PΔV = 0.
explanations.9: In an isothermal process for an ideal gas, ΔU = 0, so the first law (ΔU = Q - W) simplifies to Q = W.
explanations.10: In an adiabatic process Q = 0, so ΔU = -W; expansion (positive W) results in a decrease in internal energy and temperature.
explanations.11: The text states that the work done on or by a system equals the area under the curve on a PV diagram.
explanations.12: The first expression of the second law is that heat transfer occurs spontaneously from higher- to lower-temperature bodies.
explanations.13: Efficiency is calculated as Eff = 1 - (Qc / Qh), which is 1 - (300/500) = 0.40 or 40%.
explanations.14: The second law forbids the complete conversion of heat into work in a cyclical process (Qc cannot be zero).
explanations.15: The power stroke is represented by an adiabatic expansion (path CD) where the gas does work on the outside world.
explanations.16: Efficiency is related to the ratio of heat transferred; lower cold-path temperatures and higher hot-path temperatures increase the area inside the PV loop.
